---
title: "The infrastructure already exists. Europe never connected it"
date: 2026-06-19
draft: true
description: "Europe has already funded every piece of open-science infrastructure it needs. Two policy actions — not more technology — would turn those parts into a system that can actually reform research assessment."
categories: [Opinions, Scholarly Communication]
image: assets/era-forum-2026-essay.svg
---

For too long we have been assessing science not by what scientists say but by where they say it. Not by the content, but by the package. We have rewarded quantity over quality, precisely what ther business model of the publishing industry demands. The result is the proliferation of vast amounts of junk science that keep the industry running at the expense of our [capabaility] to trust science. Today, the noise is so loud that the quality signal is easily lost. Any honest scientist should admit that a journal brand -or indexing status- is not anymore a valid indicator to split [... from hay]. Far from an opinion, this assertion is backed by solid empirical evidence [link to meta-analisis- brembs]. This is why the research-assessment reform now under way — driven by initiatives such as CoARA — should not be regarded merely as a bureaucratic or administrative exercise. Rather research assessment reform should be regarded as a unique opportunity to restore trust in science not ofc course for the benefit of scientists but fore and foremost for the society that deserves to trust the science it pays for.

My argument here is that the the open infrastructrures necessary for supporting a meaningful research assessment reform, already exist. However, a significant for sustaining we are far closer to being able to do this than is generally recognised. The obstacle is not technology, because the technology already exists. The obstacle is that we have never connected it.

## The good news, and the bad news

The good news is genuinely good. Decades of sustained European investment have produced a remarkable range of open-science infrastructure: institutional repositories at nearly every university; Zenodo, a generalist archive for anyone without one; OpenAIRE, which aggregates and links it all; COAR Notify, ORE, and a series of open-metrics projects. Whatever component you might want for an open, repository-based system of scholarly communication and evaluation, Europe has already funded it.

The bad news is that almost none of it is visible. Ask researchers in any discipline and most will not recognise a single one of these names. They do not know OpenAIRE. They do not know they have a repository in their own institution. The reason is embarrassingly simple: at present, none of these infrastructures matters for how researchers are evaluated. They are invisible because they are irrelevant to the one thing that governs a scientific career.

The situation is as if we had built all the parts of a car — the engine, the wheels, the seats, the steering wheel — and then never assembled them. We laid the components out on the floor and admired them, but we never built the thing that takes you somewhere. That is roughly where European open-science policy stands today: a fortune spent on parts, and no car. The remedy is not another part. It is assembly, and assembly turns out to be a question of policy rather than engineering.

## Where the commercial publishers fit in

Before turning to the policy, it is worth asking why the parts were never assembled, because the answer is not an accident of bureaucracy.

When an obsolete system keeps winning despite better alternatives, it is usually because someone profits from the *status quo* and has the means to protect it. In scholarly publishing that someone is the small group of commercial publishers who have captured the function of quality certification and built extraordinarily profitable businesses around it — Elsevier's operating margins of around 37% are the figure most often cited, and they are higher than those of most industries one could name. We hand these publishers our manuscripts, our editorial labour, and our peer reviews for free; they sell the result back to our own libraries. What they sell is not access, because our work is already public the moment it leaves the lab. What they sell is prestige. The journal name is a certificate, and they own the certificate.

This is not a story about wicked capitalists and virtuous scholars. It is simpler than that. An outdated intermediary sits between research and its evaluation, extracting rent and, more damagingly, shaping what we study and how we report it to fit what sells. Every piece of the open infrastructure Europe has built is, in effect, a component of the machine that would make that intermediary unnecessary. Which is precisely why leaving the parts unassembled is so convenient for those who benefit from things as they are. Assembling the car is not a neutral technical upgrade; it is the step that removes the publisher's hold on certification.

## Assembling the car: three workflows, two actions

How, then, do the parts fit together? The interactive diagram below lays out the whole system on a single screen; the same diagram structured [my talk at the EU ERA Forum](era-forum-talk-2026.qmd), and it rewards a few minutes of exploration.

<div style="margin: 2rem 0; border-radius: 8px; overflow: hidden; box-shadow: 0 4px 24px rgba(0,0,0,0.15);">
  <iframe
    src="/talks/era-forum-2026/"
    style="width: 100%; aspect-ratio: 16/9; border: none; display: block;"
    title="From open infrastructures to research assessment — interactive diagram"
    loading="lazy"
    allowfullscreen>
  </iframe>
</div>

<p style="font-size:0.85em; color: var(--bs-secondary-color); text-align:center; margin-top:-1rem;">
  <em>Three workflows — <strong>A. Archive</strong>, <strong>B. Evaluate</strong>, <strong>C. Aggregate</strong> — built on the research community at the base and topped by OpenAIRE; the two policy actions are the boxes that switch the whole thing on. Click the nodes and the example boxes to follow the argument.</em>
  &ensp;·&ensp;
  <a href="/talks/era-forum-2026/" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Open in new tab ↗</a>
</p>

The diagram shows three layers — one to **archive** research, one to **evaluate** it, one to **aggregate** everything into a single portal — resting on a foundation that matters more than any of them: the research community itself. The scholars who produce the science are the ones who will have to drive the car. Without them, nothing above them means anything.

The system runs on three workflows.

**A — Archive.** Researchers deposit their outputs — manuscripts, but also data, code, protocols, figures, anything that documents a result — into an open repository: their institution's, or Zenodo if they have none. Nothing works unless this happens; self-archiving is the fuel that moves the entire system.

Getting it to happen has always been the difficult part. The European Commission and national governments have issued mandate after mandate requiring deposit of publicly funded research, and adoption has remained stubbornly low. Then Spain ran an unintended experiment. In 2024 the national evaluation and accreditation agency, ANECA, declared that only research deposited in open repositories would count in its assessments. Deposit rates were transformed almost overnight. There was no new technology and no new infrastructure — a single change to the rules of evaluation, and the fuel began to flow.

**B — Evaluate.** Funding agencies, evaluation committees, and scholarly societies assess research and researchers. Today those assessments are made and then discarded. The review of a paper, or of a candidate, is written, used once, and locked in a drawer; the next committee that must evaluate the same work starts again from zero. For a system that runs on bibliometrics, that may not matter. But CoARA's central commitment is to base assessment on expert peer review rather than on journal proxies, and if that is the goal, then the reviews themselves have to flow through the system as openly as the works they assess.

This, too, already exists at small scale. The Spanish Society for Experimental Psychology publishes *Psicológica*, a journal whose entire content lives in DIGITAL.CSIC, a major European repository run by the Spanish National Research Council. The society's members review submissions and deposit the full text of their reviews back into the same repository that holds the articles. The result is a diamond open-access journal — free to read, free to publish in — with open reviews, no commercial publisher anywhere in the chain, and a cost we estimated at roughly thirty euros per article in a *Nature* correspondence. The whole cycle, end to end, with the publisher removed and nothing essential lost.

**C — Aggregate.** Repository contents are harvested and exposed through a portal such as OpenAIRE, which is designed to identify peer reviews and link them, through appropriate metadata, to the works they assess. Once reviews flow openly through the system, this layer becomes the foundation for genuinely new forms of assessment — qualitative and quantitative — built on expert judgement rather than on journal metrics. But it only works when the system is connected and powered by freely flowing works and reviews.

Three workflows, then. To switch them on, two policy actions.

1. **Evaluate only what is openly deposited.** Funding, hiring, and promotion decisions should consider only outputs deposited in OpenAIRE-compliant open repositories. This is not speculative; ANECA has shown that it works, and works quickly. It is the lever that drives self-archiving.
2. **Mandate open peer review.** Any review produced in a publicly funded evaluation — of a grant, a hire, a promotion — must be deposited as an open, citable, PID-assigned object. This asks for nothing new in kind. *Psicológica* already does it; the action simply generalises a proven practice.

It is worth being clear about who I am addressing. These are levers for funders and institutions — the people who already hold the power to change the rules. I am not asking early-career researchers to be heroes. They are the most exposed people in the system, and the right thing for them to do is whatever it takes to survive within the rules as they stand. Reform is owed by those who set the rules, not by those who are punished by them.

The first action is something any funder or agency could mandate tomorrow, alongside the journal system as it stands: authors can still submit wherever they like, and evaluation simply looks at the open version. The second begins to make the open version self-sufficient. Once the quality signal lives openly on the deposited work, the journal's certificate becomes redundant, and venues like *Psicológica*, where review is open from the start, stop being curiosities and become the obvious way to do things.

## Give the parts a reason to exist

I have spent twenty years arguing for this, with concrete projects rather than manifestos, and I have arrived at a plain conclusion. These two actions are enough to give meaning to everything Europe has already built. Without them — without researchers committing their works and their reviews to the open system — that infrastructure remains invisible and effectively useless, however much more we spend on it.

We can keep funding new infrastructure and polishing the old. None of it will matter until we give the parts a reason to be used. Two policy actions assemble the car, and the destination — a science we can actually trust — is the whole point of the exercise.
